topic: reflecting on passing the journeyman exam

turns.content: man so I been thinkin about this a lot lately and I just gotta say becomin a journeyman electrician was one of the best things ive ever done with my life
That's awesome, congratulations! Passing the journeyman exam is a huge deal — it takes a lot of hours and dedication to get there. What's got you reflecting on it right now?

I was at a job site today and the inspector was askin me questions about the panel work and I just knew everything bro like it was second nature and I remembered back when I was an apprentice and I woulda been nervous as hell for that same conversation

That's a great feeling — when the knowledge just flows out of you without having to think about it. That's the difference between studying for a test and actually knowing your craft. Inspectors can tell too, when someone really knows their stuff versus someone who's just winging it. How long did it take you to get to that point?

I been doin electrical work since I got outta high school so like 6 years as an apprentice learnin everything and then I passed the exam last year. honestly when I passed I almost cried bro I studied so hard for that thing I was stressed outta my mind

Six years of grinding as an apprentice and then all that studying — that's no joke. The journeyman exam is legitimately hard, so being stressed about it is totally normal. The fact that you put in the work and came through says a lot about you. Has it changed things for you career-wise since you passed?

bro night and day. I can pull permits now on my own I can run jobs without someone checkin my work every five minutes and my pay went up which is obviously huge. I feel like people treat me different too like the other guys on the crew actually listen to me now

All of that makes sense. The license gives you authority on the job site that you just can't get any other way, no matter how skilled you are. Being able to pull your own permits and run jobs independently — that's a whole different level of responsibility and trust. And the respect from other guys on the crew naturally follows when you've got the credentials to back up the knowledge. Did the pay bump end up being significant?

yeah its solid I dont wanna say exact numbers but its like a real noticeable difference in my check every week. enough that im actually able to plan for stuff now instead of just livin paycheck to paycheck you know

That financial breathing room is one of the most underrated parts of moving up in the trades. Going from paycheck to paycheck to actually being able to plan ahead changes your whole outlook. A lot of people don't understand how well the trades can pay once you get your license and put in the time. Are you thinking about going for your master electrician license eventually?

eventually yeah for sure thats the goal. you need like 2 more years of experience as a journeyman before you can sit for the master exam so im just stackin hours right now and learnin as much as I can. my dad has his master license and he said when im ready hell help me study for it

Having your dad as a mentor for the master exam is a huge advantage — he's already been through it and knows exactly what to expect. Two more years of journeyman experience will fly by, especially if you're running your own jobs and getting exposed to different types of work. The master license would open up even more doors for you. You'd be able to supervise other electricians, take on bigger contracts, and basically run things yourself.

yeah thats what gets me fired up bro like just knowin theres a next level to get to keeps me goin. honestly some days on the job are brutal like youre in a crawl space in the middle of summer or runnin wire in the freezin cold but then I think about where im headed and it makes it worth it

That mindset is exactly what separates people who make it in the trades from people who burn out. Every job has those miserable days, but having a long-term vision keeps you pushing through the crawl spaces and the cold. And the tough days are building your experience too — every type of job you work on now is making you better for when you're running your own crew or your own business someday.

thats the dream bro. sometimes I think about how kids in high school used to clown me for goin into the trades instead of college and now im makin good money doin somethin I actually like with no student loans. no cap I wouldnt change a thing

And honestly you're proving the point with your results. No student debt, a skilled trade that's always in demand, a clear career path to master electrician, and you genuinely enjoy the work. Not everyone can say that, college degree or not. The trades get overlooked way too much, but the people who commit to them and get licensed like you did end up in a really strong position. You should be proud of where you're at — sounds like you earned every bit of it.
